coredata.dc:title: Graphene dot armored PtMo nanosponge as a highly efficient and stable electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reactions in both acidic and alkaline media
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Van-Toan
Nguyen, Ngoc-Anh
Ali, Yousuf
Tran, Quoc Chinh
Choi, Ho-Suk
coredata.dc:description: A new type of three-dimensional PtMo nanosponge wrapped with graphene dots (abbreviated as 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene) has been developed for catalyzing the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The graphene-dot wrapping takes place via the exfoliation of carbon dots during the co-reduction of Pt/Mo precursors and carbon dots at a low temperature in an aqueous solution. The synergistic effects of inner metallic alloy networks and outer graphene dots in the 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene are responsible for the outstanding HER performance and superb durability in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes. The 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene shows a low Tafel slope of 32 mV/dec and 51 mV/dec together with a low overpotential of 32 mV and 97 mV at a current density of −10 mA cm−2 in acidic and alkaline media, respectively. The catalyst also shows superb stability, as confirmed by a large amount of repeated potential cycling exceeding 5000 cycles in acidic solution and long-term durability of over 50,000 seconds in an alkaline solution. This work provides a new feasible mean of synthesizing graphene-dot-wrapped Pt-based nanostructures with highly efficient catalytic activity and superb stability for HER without the need for surfactants or stabilizers.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic exfoliation mechanism for the 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene formation process. (b) SEM image of Pt75Mo25@graphene. (c,d) TEM and HR-TEM images of Pt75Mo25@graphene. (e) HAADF-STEM and (f) EDS elemental mapping images of Pt75Mo25@graphene: (g) overlapped image, (h) Pt, (i) Mo and (j) C. (k) HR-TEM images of Pt75Mo25@graphene showing a lattice fringe image of Pt75Mo25@graphene; the inset in Fig. 1h shows the XRD pattern of the Pt75Mo25@graphene. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns of different PtMo@graphenes and XPS high-resolution scans of (b) Pt 4f, (c) Mo 3d and (d) C 1s electrons of Pt75Mo25@graphene. (e) Raman spectrum of the Pt75Mo25@graphene. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical behaviors of PtMo@graphenes in N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. Cyclic voltammograms of different 3D-porous PtMo@graphenes and cPt/C in (a) N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. (b) ECSA of the different catalysts. (c) EIS Nyquist plots recorded on various 3D-porous PtMo@graphenes and cPt/C electrodes with the potential set to −0.1 V vs RHE. The inset in Fig. 3c shows the first semicircle of Nyquist plots of various catalysts. (d) CV curves of the Pt75Mo25@graphene at the first cycle (black) and after 5000 cycles (red). The inset in Fig. 3d shows an HR-TEM image of Pt75Mo25@graphene after an accelerated durability test of 5000 cycles. All CV experiments were performed at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) LSV polarization curves of different PtxMo1-x@graphenes and cPt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4. (b) LSV curves of different PtxMo1-x@graphenes and cPt/C in a 0.1 M KOH solution. (c) The corresponding HER Tafel plots of porous Pt75Mo25@graphene, cPt/C, and others in 0.5 M H2SO4. (d) Tafel plots of porous Pt75Mo25@graphene, Pt/C, and others in 0.1 M KOH. (e) LSV curves on the Pt75Mo25@graphene surface at the first cycle (black) and after 5000 cycles (red) (in 0.5 M H2SO4). (f) Chronoamperometric responses of Pt75Mo25@graphene and Pt/C at η = −350 mV in 0.1 M KOH. The inset in Fig. 4f shows an enlarged time-dependent current density curve of the H2 bubbles formed on the Pt75Mo25@graphene surface. All LSV curves are generated at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The d111 spacings and average crystallite sizes of PtxMo1-x nanosponges.
Different HER parameters of 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene and cPt/C catalysts in both 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH electrolytes.

row.entry:
Exchange current density (j0) in 0.5 M H2SO4
(mA cm−2)

head.ce:title: Graphene dot armored PtMo nanosponge as a highly efficient and stable electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reactions in both acidic and alkaline media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Chungnam National University, 220 Gung-Dong, Yuseong-Gu, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Van-Toan Nguyen, Ngoc-Anh Nguyen and Yousuf Ali contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of three-dimensional PtMo nanosponge wrapped with graphene dots (abbreviated as 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene) has been developed for catalyzing the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The graphene-dot wrapping takes place via the exfoliation of carbon dots during the co-reduction of Pt/Mo precursors and carbon dots at a low temperature in an aqueous solution. The synergistic effects of inner metallic alloy networks and outer graphene dots in the 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene are responsible for the outstanding HER performance and superb durability in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes. The 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene shows a low Tafel slope of 32 mV/dec and 51 mV/dec together with a low overpotential of 32 mV and 97 mV at a current density of −10 mA cm−2 in acidic and alkaline media, respectively. The catalyst also shows superb stability, as confirmed by a large amount of repeated potential cycling exceeding 5000 cycles in acidic solution and long-term durability of over 50,000 seconds in an alkaline solution. This work provides a new feasible mean of synthesizing graphene-dot-wrapped Pt-based nanostructures with highly efficient catalytic activity and superb stability for HER without the need for surfactants or stabilizers.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) have received a considerable amount of attention due to the renewable and environmentally friendly methods for producing hydrogen gas fuel [1–4]. The most important approach with regard to the hydrogen evolution reaction is the electrocatalytic water-splitting method owing to its high energy conversion efficiency [5–7]. An efficient electrocatalyst is a key ingredient for the development of Pt-based electrocatalysts with high specific activity, durability and reduced Pt loading, all of which are particularly significant for the commercialization of hydrogen generation [8–10]. A three-dimensional (3D) porous nanosponge presents a particular multifunctional catalytic nano-architecture and shows excellent potential for application to electrocatalysis due to its interconnected nanoscale skeleton and voids. To date, significant effort has been devoted to the synthesis of porous Pt-based nanosponges with large surface areas, controlled porosity levels and a durable morphology. For instance, various porous nanosponges of Pt-based alloys, including PtCu, PtAu, and PtRu, have been fabricated [11–13]. However, previous methods require not only surfactants (Pluronic F127) or toxic organic solvents (hexane) but also require a high temperature for the synthesis step [11–13]. Therefore, the fabrication of porous Pt-based nanosponges without the use of a surfactant remains a key challenge in the field of electrocatalytic materials.

Among porous Pt-based nanosponges, the PtMo porous nanosponge is regarded as one of the best catalyst systems to obtain both high catalytic activity and stability for electrocatalysts [14,15], as Mo in the PtMo system promotes the formation of hydroxides and oxidizes poisons, restoring the occupied active sites [14,15]. In order to optimize the role of Mo, therefore, rational designs of PtMo porous nanosponges through nanotechnology are crucial. The 3D porous PtMo nanosponge has abundant interconnected ligaments which serve as excellent catalytic sites for the HER. Moreover, the extensive porous network structure not only facilitates the mass transfer of reactants from the exterior surface to the interior structure but also provides continuous pathways for rapid electron transport. This imparts in the catalyst significantly enhanced catalytic activity. The approaches reported thus far, however, were carried out under rigorous conditions which included high temperatures and the use of a surfactant. In several studies, the 3D porous PtMo nanosponges developed were not coated with graphene, leading to significant losses in their long-term durability [14,15]. In general, graphene-wrapped nanoparticles are prepared using metal-organic frameworks or carbon-based materials (e.g., carbon black, CNT, or graphene) [16–23]. However, the large-scale preparation of graphene-wrapped nanoparticles requires a processing temperature as high as 600 °C or more, also requiring a very complex process [16,20–23]. Therefore, a reduction in the graphene wrapping temperature remains a critical challenge to be overcome before these materials can be used as electrocatalytic materials.

Herein, we report the large-scale synthesis of a 3D graphene-wrapped PtxMo1-x nanosponge catalyst via the exfoliation of carbon dots into graphene dots during the co-reduction of Pt/Mo precursors in an aqueous solution at 70 °C for 3 h. The 3D PtxMo1-x nanosponges are self-supported interconnected network structures and are coated with thin layers of graphene dots during the reaction. By tuning the ratio of Pt/Mo in the feeding solution, PtMo alloys with adjustable compositions are easily obtained. The 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge is expected to exhibit remarkable HER performance with a low Tafel slope, a high exchange current density, and superior long-term durability in both acidic and alkaline media.

Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6 x 6H2O) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich; molybdenum (V) chloride (MoCl5, 99.6%, metal basis) was provided by Alfa Aesar. All materials were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Commercial Pt/C (activated carbon supported Pt nanoparticles, 20 wt% Pt) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and was employed as a benchmark material for comparison. Deionized water with resistivity exceeding 18.0 MΩcm from a JL–RO 100 Millipore-Q Plus purifier was used throughout the experiments.

The synthesis of the 3D-porous PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponge was carried out by reducing Pt and Mo precursors with an aqueous NaBH4 solution in the presence of an appropriate amount of carbon dots. The carbon dots act not only as a structure-directing agent but also as a carbon source to cover the 3D-porous PtxMo1-x nanosponge [24,25]. To prepare Pt50Mo50@graphene as an example, a 5 mL aqueous solution containing 20 mM H2PtCl6 and 20 mM MoCl5 was quickly added to 195 mL of H2O containing 28 mg of carbon dots. After sonicating the solution for 30 min, 20 mL of the aqueous 50 mM NaBH4 reductant was slowly injected into the as-prepared homogeneous solution under vigorous stirring. The stirring was continued at 70 °C for 3 h until the entire solution became black. The mixture was then centrifuged, washed with DI water, and dried at 70 °C for further use. Using a similar method, Pt75Mo25@graphene, Pt90Mo10@grapheneand Pt25Mo75@graphene were fabricated by tuning the atomic ratios of Pt/Mo in the feeding solution with the same amount of carbon dots.

The suspension of the catalyst was prepared by dispersing 1.8 mg of the as-prepared 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene in 1 mL of a mixed water/isopropanol(IPA)/Nafion ionomer solution (80/20/0.02, %v). After ultrasonication for 1 h, 3 μL of the mixture solution was dropped onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode, with the mass loading of the catalysts set to 76 μg cm−2. The working electrodes were dried under an alcohol environment.

All of the electrochemical measurements were conducted in a conventional three-electrode configuration at 298 K equipped with a rotating disk electrode (RDE). A modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d = 3 mm, RRDE-3A Rotating Ring Disk Electrode Apparatus, Japan) was used as the working electrode, and platinum wire was used as the counter electrode. Ag/AgCl and Hg/HgO were used as the reference electrodes in the acidic and alkaline media, respectively. All potentials are reported after conversion on a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The electrolytes were purged with N2 gas for 30 min prior to the measurements to remove dissolved oxygen. The electrocatalytic activities of the catalysts were carried out by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scan rate of 5 mV/s with a rotation speed of 1000 in order to remove hydrogen gas bubbles at the surface of the electrode. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed in a 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution from 0.1 to 1.2 V (vs. RHE) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on the IVIUMSTAT electrochemical interface (Innovative Electrochemical Instrumentation, The Netherlands) with a frequency ranging from 0.1 to 105 Hz. The EIS spectra were analyzed and fitted using the Z-view software.

A schematic illustration of the preparation process of the 3D-porous PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponge is shown in Fig. 1 a. The synthesis of the 3D-porous PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponge was carried out by reducing Pt and Mo precursors with an aqueous NaBH4 solution in the presence of an appropriate amount of carbon dots. Carbon dots act not only as a structure-directing agent but also as a carbon source to cover the 3D-porous PtxMo1-x nanosponge [24,25]. Fig. 1b shows a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of Pt75Mo25@graphene. A structure similar to cotton candy is characterized by a three-dimensionally interconnected network, which can also be confirmed in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1c and S1a-1c). All 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges have many branches with uniform diameters (Fig. 1c and S1a-1c). Interestingly, the morphologies of 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges prepared with different Pt/Mo molar ratios were not significantly different (Fig. 1c and S1a-1c). A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of Pt75Mo25@graphene indicates that the Pt75Mo25 is covered with transparent graphene layers (Fig. 1d). Thin carbon layers wrap the Pt75Mo25 nanosponge to form a Pt75Mo25@graphene hybrid structure. Our previous multidimensional study using experiments and DFT calculations indicated that the presence of Pt precursors in the reaction solution critically affects the exfoliation of the carbon dots into graphene dots and the subsequent formation of graphene-wrapped PtNi nanosponges [24]. Therefore, the formation of small clusters of Pt and Mo atoms inside the Cdot layers can occur during the reaction (Fig. 1a). A high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image further confirms that uniform metal nanopores are formed (Fig. 1e). The corresponding element mapping of the Pt75Mo25@graphene indicates that both Pt and Mo atoms are homogeneously distributed throughout the porous structure (Fig. 1f–j), confirming the alloy structure of PtMo. The elemental C atoms reveal a uniform distribution of carbon in the nanoporous structure of Pt75Mo25@graphene (Fig. 1j). Fig. 1k clearly shows that lattice spacings of 0.231 and 0.195 nm are consistent with the (111) and (200) planes of the cubic Pt phase, respectively. However, no lattice spacing of metallic Mo is found, implying the formation of PtMo alloy which retains the crystal structure of metallic Pt. The broad peak centered around 23° is associated with the (002) plane of the hexagonal structure of graphene dots, confirming the coexistence of graphene layers together with PtMo alloy (see the inset in Fig. 1k) [26].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out to elucidate the valence states and compositions of the Pt75Mo25@graphene catalyst. The Pt 4f spectrum of Pt75Mo25@graphene (Fig. 2b) was deconvoluted into four peaks. The binding energy levels of Pt 4f were found to be approximately 71.2 eV and 74.5 eV, corresponding to platinum in the zero-valent state. The second and weaker doublet at 72.2 eV and 75.9 eV can be assigned to oxidized Pt in divalent states of PtO or Pt(OH)2 [28]. Metallic states of Pt in XPS coincide with the XRD pattern, which shows the (fcc) phase of Pt. The deconvolution of the Mo 3d energy level signals revealed in Fig. 2c and Table S1. The peaks at 232.6 eV and 236.0 eV characterize MoO3, which are attributable to Mo6+ 3d5/3 and Mo6+ 3d3/2, respectively. The lower energy peaks at 229.4 eV and 233.2 eV are assignable to Mo4+ 3d5/2 and Mo4+ 3d3/2 of MoO2, respectively [29–31]. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum is also shown in Fig. 2d, with the main peak at 284.6 eV confirming that the graphite carbon is the most common species [31]. The Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 peaks of high-resolution Pt 4f XPS spectra in Pt75Mo25@graphene were shifted to higher binding energy compared to the Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 peaks in Pt 4f of as-synthesized Pt75Mo25 without using Cdot (Fig. S2). According to these XPS results, there exists a strong interaction between Pt75Mo25 nanosponge and graphene, which supports to transfer electrons from the surface of Pt75Mo25 nanosponge to the surface of graphene dot layers [32,33]. Moreover, the mass Pt: Mo surface ratio was 95.5 to 4.5, as calculated from the intensity of the corresponding peaks, which was approximately 7.1 times larger than the initial theoretical value (3:1) of the atomic ratio of precursors. The migration of Pt onto the topmost surface generally occurred during a reduction process [34]. As a result, the surface of Pt75Mo25 alloy particles becomes enriched with Pt atoms.

The Raman spectrum was measured to confirm the presence of carbon. The characteristic carbon Raman bands contain the G band and the D band, with G band confirming the existence of the sp2-hybridized (crystalline) type and the D band accounting for defects and disorders associated with amorphous carbon. Fig. 2e shows the Raman spectrum of the Pt75Mo25@graphene material, including the two peaks of the G band (∼ 1575 cm−1) and the D band (∼ 1380 cm−1). The smaller ID/IG ratio (∼0.5) of exfoliated carbon in the Pt75Mo25@graphene material was compared to the ID/IG ratio (∼0.67) of Cdots from a previous paper [24,25]. The smaller ID/IG ratio is attributed to the conversion of partially sp3-hybridized carbon into sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon. This explains the formation of graphene dots as a result of the exfoliation and reduction of carbon dots [24,25].

To understand the electrochemical behaviors of the PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges, CV studies were conducted using an N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 3a and S6a. The CV of Pt75Mo25@graphene shows the highest charge of oxidative and reductive peaks for proton adsorption/desorption on the surface of the catalyst. It may be easier to reach a state of equilibrium between the adsorbed and desorbed protons at the Pt75Mo25@graphene surface compared to other catalysts. The anodic peaks at E ≈ 0.85 V correspond to the formation of oxide groups on the PtMo surface and the associated corresponding cathodic peak at 0.77 V is evidence of the reduced peaks of the PtMo surface oxides. The ECSA values for all catalysts to be compared were calculated, as displayed in Fig. 3 b. The ECSA values of the Pt75Mo25@graphene, Pt90Mo10@graphene, Pt50Mo50@graphene, Pt25Mo75@graphene and commercial Pt/C (cPt/C) were determined to be approximately 67.5, 63.1, 59.2, 56.5 and 58.1 m2g-1, respectively. The highest ECSA of the Pt75Mo25@graphene is ascribed to the porous morphology and the optimization of the stoichiometric ratio of Pt to Mo, leading to the maximization of the electrochemically active sites of Pt75Mo25@graphene.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz at overpotential of η = 100 mV in a 0.1M KOH solution is performed. Typical Nyquist plots of Pt90Mo10@graphene, Pt75Mo25@graphene, Pt50Mo50@graphene, Pt25Mo75@graphene and cPt/C in the 0.1 M KOH electrolyte are shown in Fig. 3c. Two semicircles are observed on the Nyquist plots of all catalysts, indicating a two-time constant model and the porous nature of the electrode (Fig. S3) [3,35,36]. The resistance related to the surface porosity (Rp) of various PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges appears in Fig. S3, confirming that a porous network structure of PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponge is formed through exfoliation of Cdot layers. The porous morphology of PtxMo1-x@graphenes is consistent with the TEM image and was confirmed in Fig. 1. The charge-transfer resistance value (Rct) of Pt75Mo25@graphene (17.3 Ω) is much smaller than those of cPt/C (48.05 Ω) and Pt25Mo75@graphene (117.3 Ω). The superb electron conduction, through a porous structure resulting from strong chemical interaction between inner PtMo nanosponge and outer graphene dot layers, is responsible for the smaller Rct, leading to accelerating the HER kinetics on the surface of Pt75Mo25@graphene. Hence, the Pt75Mo25@graphene catalyst exhibits faster charge transferability and higher catalytic activity for the HER. The resistance related to the surface porosity (Rp) values of the Pt75Mo25@graphene (5.5 Ω), cPt/C (6.7 Ω), Pt50Mo50@graphene (7.62 Ω), and Pt25Mo75@graphene (9.3 Ω) samples (see the inset of in Fig. 3c) indicate that the electron transport through the Pt75Mo25@graphene is much faster than those of the others. The details of the EIS calculations are described in Table S2 in the Supporting Information. Fig. 3d shows the changes in the CV curves before and after an accelerated durability test of 5000 cycles. After 5000 cycles, the measured degradation of the ECSA of Pt75Mo25@graphene is approximately 15%. On the other hand, the cPt/C catalyst shows more than 40% loss in the ECSA result (Fig. S4), demonstrating that Pt75Mo25@graphene has better durability than cPt/C. The inset of Fig. 3d shows an HRTEM image of Pt75Mo25@graphene after an accelerated durability test of 5000 cycles. The porous structure of the Pt75Mo25 alloy and the encapsulation of the Pt75Mo25 alloy by graphene layers are well maintained even after running 5000 cycles of CV. Therefore, it can be confirmed that 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene possesses superior stability.

The electrochemical catalytic activities of the various PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges and cPt/C samples with regard to the HER were recorded using a typical three-electrode electrochemical cell in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH electrolytes with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the geometric current density in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 for various PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges and cPt/C catalysts are depicted in Fig. 4a and S6b. The cPt/C catalyst exhibits high activity towards the HER in acidic media with an onset potential of −18 mV. The 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene also reveals excellent HER activity with a small onset potential of −11 mV and a very small overpotential (η10) of 32 mV at a current density of −10 mA/cm2. In contrast, Pt25Mo75@graphene, Pt50Mo50@graphene and cPt/C need a larger η10 value to drive the current density of −10 mA/cm2, with corresponding values of 47 mV, 40 mV and 42 mV. The results of a comparison of the HER performances of Pt75Mo25@graphene with those of other catalysts are also summarized in Table 2 and Table S3. Clearly, Pt75Mo25@graphene displays superior HER catalytic activities stemming from the optimization of the atomic Pt/Mo ratio together with tuning the lattice parameter of PtxMo1-x@graphene compared to the other catalysts tested here [27,37,38]. Especially, the overpotential value of Pt75Mo25@graphene at a current density of 100 mA/cm2 is 56.0 mV lower than that of Pt75Mo25 nanoalloy without Cdot as shown in Fig. S7. This result is good evidence of better performance for HER of Pt75Mo25@graphene compared to Pt75Mo25 nanoalloy without Cdot. Armoring graphene dot layers on the surface of Pt75Mo25 nanosponge can be responsible for the better performance of Pt75Mo25@graphene because these layers enrich the electron density at interface between Pt75Mo25 nanosponge and the tiny surface of armoring graphene dot shell [39]. Subsequently, this is beneficial for reactions of Volmer step H+ to adsorbed H+ (H+ + e− → H+ ad) and Heyrovsky step H+ to H2 gas (H+ + Had + e− → H2) on the surface of Pt75Mo25@graphene, leading to accelerating the rate of hydrogen evolution reaction.

The HER performance was also investigated in a 0.1 M KOH medium to assess the versatility of the 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge catalyst. Fig. 4 b exhibits the LSV curves of various 3D-porous PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponges and cPt/C in N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The cPt/C catalyst also reveals the best HER catalytic activity with a very small onset potential of −28 mV. However, 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge catalyst exhibits an onset potential of −25 mV, which is slightly smaller than that of cPt/C. To compare the HER activities exhibited by all catalysts tested, the overpotential (η10) required to drive a current density of −10 mA cm−2 is also selected as a reference. For the 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge, η10 ≈ 97 mV is utilized to produce a current density at −10 mA cm−2 in 0.1 M KOH, which is 31 mV less overpotential than that of cPt/C (Table 2). The Tafel slope is one of the most important parameters for the evaluation of the HER mechanism and for finding the rate-determining step. Tafel plots of cPt/C, Pt25Mo75@graphene, Pt50Mo50@graphene and Pt75Mo25@graphene in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 are presented in Fig. 4c and Table 2. The Tafel slope of cPt/C is of 36 mV/dec, close to the theoretical value, whereas Pt75Mo25@graphene exhibits a smaller Tafel slope of 32 mV/dec. The lower Tafel slope suggests that the HER on the Pt75Mo25@graphene follows the Volmer-Tafel mechanism, in which the adsorption of the hydrogen atom is the rate-determining step to produce H2 gas [40]. Furthermore, the important kinetics parameter for HER performance is an inherent exchange current density related to the speed of electron transfer under reversible conditions. The exchange current density (j0) is 0.91 mA cm−2 for Pt75Mo25@graphene and 0.75 mA cm−2 for cPt/C. The higher exchange current density implies that the rate of electron transfer through Pt75Mo25@graphene is faster than that of cPt/C, leading to effectively improving the HER performance of Pt75Mo25@graphene. Tafel analyses of all catalysts tested in 0.1 M KOH were also carried out, as shown in Fig. 4d. The 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge has a Tafel slope value of 51 mV/dec, while the slope value of cPt/C was found to be 59 mV/dec. Thus, the 3D-porous Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge electrocatalyst easily surpasses the cPt/C catalyst in an alkaline medium based on the onset potential, overpotential (η10) and Tafel slope.

To assess the long-term durability of the catalyst for the HER, the LSV curve and CV curves of the working electrode were obtained in N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 at a scan speed of 5 mV/s (Fig. 4e, Figs. S5 and S8). The HER performance of Pt75Mo25@graphene as indicated by the onset potential and current density at any overpotential exhibits little loss after 5000 cycles, suggesting the excellent long-term stability of the Pt75Mo25@graphene catalyst. After 5000 cycles, the measured degradation of ECSA of the cPt/C catalyst exceeds 25% in terms of the ECSA value (Fig. S5a), while the ECSA loss of Pt75Mo25@graphene is approximately 10% (Fig. S5b). In addition, Fig. 4f shows the chronoamperometric responses of Pt75Mo25@graphene and cPt/C to compare their time-dependent current density changes as measured at an overpotential of η = 350 mV. The current density of cPt/C reveals a significant loss within 10,000 s of the chronoamperometric measurements. In contrast, the current density of the Pt75Mo25@graphene catalyst reveals little loss within 5000 s, and this sample remains mostly stable for more than 50,000 s. As shown in the inset in Fig. 4f, numerous released H2 bubbles were clearly observed during the chronoamperometric measurements. The time-dependent current density curve in the inset exhibits a zigzag shape due to the alternating processes of bubble accumulation and bubble release. Hence, the superior stability of the Pt75Mo25@graphene catalyst in both acidic and alkaline media must stem from the graphene shell layers on the surface of the 3D-porous Pt75Mo25 nanosponge. These graphene dot layers protect the Pt75Mo25 nanosponge networks from dissolution, corrosion, agglomeration and poisoning under long-term reaction operation [41].

We developed a facile synthesis method by which to create a 3D PtxMo1-x@graphene nanosponge at a low temperature (70 °C) without using any surfactants or stabilizers. The HER activity of the 3D Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge was superior to that of the commercial Pt/C catalyst in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes. The 3D Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge showed low overpotentials of 32 mV and 97 mV while producing H2 gas at a current density of −10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH, respectively. The stability of the 3D Pt75Mo25@graphene nanosponge catalyst was also much better than that of commercial cPt/C in both acidic and alkaline media. The superior stability was confirmed in a test involving a large amount of repeated potential cycling with more than 5000 cycles in an acidic solution, with long-term durability exceeding 50,000 seconds in an alkaline solution. The outstanding activity and excellent durability of 3D Pt75Mo25@graphene must be due to the unique 3D nanosponge structure wrapped with graphene dots, leading to a synergistic effect between the PtMo alloy networks and the graphene dots. This study thus provides a new route by which to develop highly efficient robust Pt-based electrocatalysts for HER applications by tuning the morphologies of metallic nano-alloys while also wrapping them with layers of graphene dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a National Research Foundation (NRF) grants (NRF-2017R1A2B2001911, NRF-2017R1A4A1015360) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.01.087.
